coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots driven directional charge migration on sulfur vacancy enriched ZnIn2S4 nanosheets for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Kai
Li, Zenan
Zhang, Tianyang
He, Tiwei
Fan, Zhenglong
Huang, Hui
Liao, Fan
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur vacancy as a kind of intrinsic defect for metal sulfides plays an important role in adjusting the electronic structure, but the uncontrollability of the regulation does not always lead to efficient activity in photocatalytic hydrogen production. To address this issue, a photocatalytic nanocomposite is designed and constructed by in situ inserting carbon dots (CDs) into ultrathin ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) nanosheets with sulfur vacancies (CDs/Vs-ZIS) by a one-step hydrothermal method. The charge transfer kinetics analysis of CDs/Vs-ZIS demonstrates that the trapped electrons by sulfur vacancies are directed transferred to the surface of CDs. The electron sink effect leads to further enhancement of the charge separation efficiency on CDs surface and CDs act as hydrogen-producing active sites to greatly improve the hydrogen evolution. Benefiting from the directional charge migration driven by CDs in Vs-ZIS nanosheets, the optimal CDs/Vs-ZIS photocatalyst exhibits a superior photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 5.93 mmol g−1h−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ZnIn2S4
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
Sulfur vacancy
Transient photovoltage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and energy band structure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program of China ( 2018YFE0306105 ), National Key R&D Program of China ( 2020YFA0406104 , 2020YFA0406101 ), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51821002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51972216 , 52272043 , 52271223 , 52202107 , 52201269 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20220028 , BK20190041 ), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province ( 2019B010933001 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image of CDs, (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM images of Vs-ZIS, (d) SEM, (e) TEM and (f-g) HRTEM images of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, (h) AFM image and corresponding height profile of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, (i-m) HAADF-STEM and element mapping images of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS.

(a) XRD patterns of CDs, ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, (b) upper part: FT-IR spectra of CDs and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, and bottom part: Raman spectra of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) Zn 2p, (d) In 3d and (e) S 2p for ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, (f) ESR spectra of ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, (b) Tauc plots of ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS.

(a) Time courses of photocatalytic H2 evolution over samples. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments. (b) photocatalytic H2 evolution generation rate of samples. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments. (c) wavelength dependence of AQY of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments. (d) cyclic stability experiments of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
(a) TPV curves, (b) attenuation constants (τ), (c) charge extraction rate (tmax), (d) maximum electron extraction amount (A) of CDs, ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS.

(a) FFT spectra of ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, (b) Intensity-Time curves of ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (f = 1 Hz), (c) Intensity-Time curves of ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (f = 5 Hz), and (d) Peak time difference (Δt) at different frequencies for Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (t′ and t represent the peak occurrence time of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS and Vs-ZIS, respectively).

(a) TRPL spectra, (b) PL spectra of ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, (c) transient photocurrent responses, (d) Nyquist spectra for CDs, ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (Insert is equivalent circuit model).
Schematic diagram of the mechanism of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution for 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots driven directional charge migration on sulfur vacancy enriched ZnIn2S4 nanosheets for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China (Z. Kang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Well-designed CDs-coupled sulfur vacancy enriched ZnIn2S4 nanosheet (CDs/Vs-ZIS) has been developed as an efficient photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction. The trapped photogenerated electrons by sulfur vacancies migrate directionally to the surface of CDs. The electron sink effect leads to further enhancement of the charge separation efficiency, which greatly improves the hydrogen evolution on CD active sites.

Sulfur vacancy as a kind of intrinsic defect for metal sulfides plays an important role in adjusting the electronic structure, but the uncontrollability of the regulation does not always lead to efficient activity in photocatalytic hydrogen production. To address this issue, a photocatalytic nanocomposite is designed and constructed by in situ inserting carbon dots (CDs) into ultrathin ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) nanosheets with sulfur vacancies (CDs/Vs-ZIS) by a one-step hydrothermal method. The charge transfer kinetics analysis of CDs/Vs-ZIS demonstrates that the trapped electrons by sulfur vacancies are directed transferred to the surface of CDs. The electron sink effect leads to further enhancement of the charge separation efficiency on CDs surface and CDs act as hydrogen-producing active sites to greatly improve the hydrogen evolution. Benefiting from the directional charge migration driven by CDs in Vs-ZIS nanosheets, the optimal CDs/Vs-ZIS photocatalyst exhibits a superior photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 5.93 mmol g−1h−1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar water splitting is a green and clean technology to mediate the increasingly serious energy crisis and environmental pollution [1,2]. In order to improve the photocatalytic efficiency, suitable photocatalysts with strong redox activity, effective absorption of visible light, and low cost are the most expected [3,4]. Metal sulfides appear as one of the most promising photocatalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen production [5]. Among them, ternary chalcogenide ZnIn2S4 is a representative reduction photocatalyst in the metal sulfides family due to its narrow bandgap and stable chemical properties [6]. However, pure ZnIn2S4 suffers from rapid charge recombination, leading to an inferior activity toward photocatalytic hydrogen production [7]. The excitation, migration and recombination of charge carriers in ZnIn2S4 can be controlled by modulating the in-plane surface atomic composition and electronic state.

Vacancy engineering has been widely adopted to regulate interfacial interactions of metal sulfides to promote the dynamic properties of charge carriers [8]. The vacancy can not only form defect energy levels to enhance visible light absorption, but also capture photogenerated electron to accelerate carrier separation [9,10]. The introduction of sulfur vacancies is an effective approach to modify the electronic structure and improve reaction kinetics of ZnIn2S4. For instance, Du et al. synthesized 2D ZnIn2S4 monolayer nanosheets with sulfur vacancies by a facile method with different solvents [11]. The introduction of sulfur vacancies in the unique 2D ZnIn2S4 nanosheets prolongs the carrier lifetime and improves photocatalytic efficiency. However, It is noteworthy that some reports also show that sulfur vacancies can serve as carrier recombination centers due to the unregulated defect spatial distribution and inappropriate defect concentrations and types, which ultimately leads to performance losses [12,13]. In addition, hydrogen production active sites and carrier recombination sites are always formed simultaneously due to the uncontrollability of the defect engineering strategy, resulting in intrinsic poor charge separation and mobility. Therefore, the construction of a directional charge transfer channel on the photocatalyst surface to promote charge transfer is one of the key steps to signally boost the photocatalytic reaction. Since the photoinduced charge carriers could be effectively transferred and extracted by the cocatalysts, appropriate cocatalyst modification of metal sulfides has proven to be an effective method to significantly alleviating unfavorable charge recombination and endowing the rapid charges separation and transport [14].

Carbon dots (CDs), as emerging carbon nanomaterials (size less than 10 nm), have attracted considerable interest due to low cost and unique optical properties [15]. In the meantime, the functional groups on the surface of the CDs such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide, can easily composite with other materials for photocatalytic application [16]. These advantages make CDs ideal subunits for serving as co-catalytic active sites in the construction of photocatalysts, which can act as electron acceptor/donor to promote the transfer and separation of charge [17]. For example, An et al. synthesized ultrathin CdS@CDs nanosheets through a simple and reproducible route, in which CDs accelerated the separation of charge and greatly boosted the photocatalytic reaction efficiency [18]. For instance, Shi et al. fabricated ternary MoS2/AgInZnS/ferrocene-functionalized carbon dots (MoS2/AIZS/fCDs) photocatalysts and demonstrated that the introduction of fCDs can rapidly extract holes from AIZS to suppress the electron-hole recombination [19]. Thus, the coupling of CDs with sulfur vacancies-tailored metal sulfides will tune the directional transfer of photogenerated carriers from sulfur vacancies to active sites, and effectively drive effective charge separation. However, the synergistic effect of CDs and sulfur vacancies on the photogenerated charges of ZnIn2S4 for promoting photocatalytic hydrogen evolution still lacks a comprehensive study.

Herein, well-designed CDs in situ embedded in sulfur vacancy-tailored ZnIn2S4 nanosheets (CDs/Vs-ZIS) are synthesized as highly-efficient photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution. The sulfur vacancies trap the photogenerated electrons of the 2D ZIS nanosheets and prolong the carrier lifetime. Meanwhile, in situ embedded CDs as electron sink strongly couples with 2D ZIS nanosheet, making the electrons trapped by sulfur vacancies migrate directionally to the surface of CDs, thereby accelerating charge separation and promoting hydrogen production. As a result, the as-prepared CDs/Vs-ZIS exhibits a maximum hydrogen production rate of 5.93 mmol g−1h−1 at atmospheric pressure, due to the collaborative optimization of CDs cocatalysts in Vs-ZIS nanosheets.

The morphology and microstructure of obtained samples were studied by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, CDs with diameter of approximate 5 nm can be obviously observed without agglomeration, and it presents clear lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, which can be ascribed to the (100) lattice planes of graphite [20]. The TEM image revealed that Vs-ZIS displayed typical 2D ultrathin nanosheet morphology (Fig. 1b), which was consistent with the SEM results (Fig. S1). As the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of pristine Vs-ZIS depicted in Fig. 1c, the interplanar d-spacing between lattice fringes is 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (102) planes [21]. HRTEM image also shows discontinued lattice fringes, indicating the presence of abundant vacancies defects [22]. In contrast, when 2% CDs is added in Vs-ZIS, the morphology of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS composites is almost the same as that of pure Vs-ZIS (Fig. 1d and e), indicating that there is no obvious damage to the morphology and structure of Vs-ZIS after the introduction of CDs. The lattice fringe of 0.21 nm corresponding to CDs is accurately found in Fig. 1f and g, and that of 0.32 nm corresponding to (102) planes of ZnIn2S4 are located around CDs. As described in Fig. 1g, lattice discontinuities can be seen clearly in the cyan circle of the HRTEM image, which may be caused by sulfur vacancies [22]. Furthermore, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was performed to quantify the average thickness of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS composites of approximately 2.81 nm, and the corresponding height profile is displayed in Fig. 1h, indicating the ultrathin nature of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS nanosheets. Moreover, the corresponding energy dispersive electron spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping results confirm that the Zn, In, S, and C elements in 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS are distributed uniformly (Fig. 1i). In brief, it can be verified that the CDs are successfully embedding on the surface of Vs-ZIS nanosheet.

The crystalline structure of synthesized samples was further characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD). The characteristic peaks of CDs at 2θ = 21.5° and 26.5° can be attributed to a short-range order in stacked graphene layer and graphene phase, respectively (Fig. 2 a). In addition, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 21.6°, 27.7°, 30.5°, 47.3°, 52.5° and 55.8° are well consistent with the (006), (102), (104), (110), (116), and (022) planes of hexagonal ZIS (PDF No.65–2023), indicating that ZIS and Vs-ZIS were successfully prepared. It is noteworthy that the intensity of the diffraction peaks of Vs-ZIS becomes weaker than that of ZIS, and the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak of Vs-ZIS is wider than that of ZIS, indicating that the excessive sulfur source (thioacetamide) during crystal growth leads to the generation of sulfur vacancies [23,24]. Moreover, the XRD patterns of CDs/ZIS and CDs/Vs-ZIS composites are similar to that of ZIS and Vs-ZIS due to the low content of CDs in the composites (Fig. 2a and S2), fiercely proving the integrity of the composite after the introduction of CDs.

The successful preparation of the catalyst was further verified by Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra and Raman spectra, which are displayed in Fig. 2b. The upper part of Fig. 2b presents FT-IR spectrum, as for CDs, several absorption peaks at approximately 3410, 1711, 1586, 1234, and 1022 cm−1 are assigned to the OH, CO, CC, COC, and CO vibration, respectively, demonstrating CDs with oxygen-contained functional groups have hydrophilicity [25,26]. In addition, the wettability of CDs is explored using the contact angle of water. The water contact angle of pure CDs is 26.1°, which means that the water could wet the CDs surface because of abundant surface hydrophilic functional groups (Fig. S3). The 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS exhibits the characteristic peaks around 3410, 1611, and 1400 cm−1 assigning to the bending vibration modes of the OH group [27]. Owing to the low content of CDs, the characteristic peaks belonging to CDs cannot be identified in the 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS composites, proving that the change in the chemical structure via the embedding of CDs was negligible. The bottom of Fig. 2b is Raman spectrum of CDs, the peaks at 1320 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to D mode and G mode of CDs, confirming the successful preparation of CDs [28].

The chemical structure and electronic state of samples were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS full spectra revealed the existence of Zn, In, S and C in the CDs/Vs-ZIS composites (Fig. S4), and the peak intensity of the C element in CDs/Vs-ZIS was stronger than that in ZIS and Vs-ZIS, which confirms that CDs were successfully embedded in Vs-ZIS. In the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum as shown in Fig. S5a, there are three peaks at 284.6, 286.1 and 288.8 eV corresponding to the CC, CO, and CO, respectively, which match well with the results of FT-IR. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S5b) splits up into two peaks at 532.0 and 533.2 eV, which are originate from to CO and CO, respectively [29,30]. For the high-resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p and In 3d in ZIS (Fig. 2c, d), two obvious peaks of 1022.3 and 1045.5 eV belonged to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, two peaks at 445.1 and 452.6 eV are ascribed to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively [31]. In the high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum, two main peaks at 161.7 and 162.9 eV correspond to to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 in ZIS (Fig. 2e) [31]. Compared with ZIS, the peaks position of S 2p spin orbitals in Vs-ZIS shifts toward a slight lower binding energy, as well as that of Zn 2p and In 3d, suggesting that there is low-coordination S due to the presence of more sulfur vacancies [32,33]. For 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, the binding energies of Zn 2p, In 3d, and S 2p exhibit a slight positive shift relative to the value in Vs-ZIS, suggested the decreased density of outer electrons, which indicates that the electrons transferred from the Vs-ZIS to CDs. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was also employed to evaluate sulfur vacancies and electron configuration of Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (Fig. 2f). Clearly, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS exhibits EPR signals at a g-value of 2.003, while the EPR signal of ZIS is very weak with negligible signal change, which corroborates the existence of abundant sulfur vacancies in Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, agreeing well with the results of XPS [34,35]. In particular, the signal intensity in 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is much stronger than that of Vs-ZIS, which shows that the insertion of CDs is more conducive to the generation of sulfur vacancies. Previous work has reported that when CDs are introduced, S atoms got stripped heavily, leading to the formation of vastly elongational defects and further the decrease of the quantity of Zn-S dangling bonds in the ZnIn2S4 slab, suggesting the decrease of coordination number for Zn-S and increasing unsaturated and low-coordination S sites [36–38]. Therefore, in-situ embedding of CDs induced additional sulfur vacancies into the ZIS. According to the above analysis and exposition, it not only confirms that there are sulfur vacancies in Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS composite, but also indicates the presence of electronic interactions between CDs and Vs-ZIS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and energy band structure
ce:section.ce:para: The light-adsorption properties and band structures of the as-prepared photocatalysts were investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS). As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the absorption edge of Vs-ZIS shows slight red-shift compared with pure ZIS, and pure CDs displays a broad-spectral photon absorption capability in the ranging from 300 to 800 nm. When sulfur vacancies are introduced into ZIS, the absorption tail of Vs-ZIS is significantly higher than that of ZIS. Reported literature indicates that the absorption tails are generally related to various defects, demonstrating the presence of abundant sulfur vacancies, which enhances the optical absorption of ZIS to visible light [39]. Compared with Vs-ZIS, 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS clearly exhibits a stronger visible-light response (500–700 nm), manifesting that the introduction of CDs and sulfur vacancies can significantly improve the light absorbance. The band gap energy (Eg) of samples is estimated according to Kubelka-Munk method, and illustrated in Fig. 3b. The Eg value of ZIS is estimated as 2.60 eV. And Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS give the value of 2.57 and 2.53 eV, respectively.

The energy band structure of CDs, ZIS and Vs-ZIS were determined by Mott-Schottky plots and cyclic voltammetry (CV). The Mott-Schottky plots of ZIS and Vs-ZIS both show positive values, as shown in Fig S6a and b, implying the n-type semiconductor traits. The flat band potential (Efb) is investigated by the Mott-Schottky plots and Efb corresponds to Fermi level. The derived flat band potential (Efb) of ZIS and Vs-ZIS can be determined as − 1.18 V and − 1.25 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Generally, the conduction band (ECB) positions of ZIS and Vs-ZIS are calculated as − 1.38 and − 1.45 V vs. Ag/AgCl, which are − 1.18 and − 1.25 vs. NHE (ENHE = EAg/AgCl + E θ, pH = 6.8), respectively [40]. The valence band (EVB) potentials of ZIS and Vs-ZIS are determined to be 1.42 and 1.32 V vs. NHE at pH = 6.8. The ECB level of ZIS and Vs-ZIS is positioned above the reduction level for H+/H2 (−0.40 V vs. NHE, pH = 6.8), and the ECB of Vs-ZIS is more negative than that of ZIS, which shows great potential for photocatalytic water splitting. Meanwhile, to further determine the band structures of CDs, the CV curve of CDs is shown in Fig. S6c [41]. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) are predicted to be 1.50 and − 1.16 V vs. NHE at pH = 6.8. Therefore, the band structures of samples are displayed in Fig. S6d.

The photocatalytic hydrogen production performance was measured in the triethanolamine (TEOA) solution system under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) at atmospheric pressure. Fig. 4 a shows the hydrogen production amount of the CDs, Vs-ZIS and CDs/Vs-ZIS composites. CDs exhibit a low activity of hydrogen production due to the spanking quenching of photogenerated charge carriers, while Vs-ZIS shows higher hydrogen production capacity. The CDs/Vs-ZIS composites exhibit enhanced performance for hydrogen production compared with the pure CDs or Vs-ZIS catalyst, demonstrating the important role of the introduction of CDs. Subsequently, the influence of sulfur vacancies on photocatalytic hydrogen activity is also studied. As shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. 4b, the Vs-ZIS shows the hydrogen production rate of 2.02 mmol g−1h−1, which is approximately 3.1 times of ZIS (0.65 mmol g−1h−1), implying the significant role of sulfur vacancies in promoting photocatalytic hydrogen activity. Furthermore, the hydrogen production rate of the CDs composites is improved with CDs as the cocatalyst, and the 2% CDs/ZIS catalyst shows the optimal hydrogen generation rate of 1.37 mmol g−1h−1. As displayed in Fig. 4b, after introducing sulfur vacancies, the 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS possesses superior hydrogen production rate (5.93 mmol g−1h−1), which is almost 2.9 and 4.3 times higher than those of pristine Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/ZIS, respectively. We infer that 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS has excellent hydrogen generation ability, which is attributed to the collaborative optimization of sulfur vacancies and CDs. The exceptional activity of the as-prepared 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is significantly superior to those of most performances for ZnIn2S4-based photocatalysts as illustrated in Table S1. However, as the content of CDs in Vs-ZIS increases, the photocatalytic activity was greatly affected. It can be concluded that more loading suffers from the light shielding effect and aggregation caused by excessive additives. As presented in Fig. 4c, the calculated apparent quantum yield (AQY) values of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is in direct proportion to the light absorption intensity and the AQY at 420 nm reaches 4.61%. The stability and reusability tests of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS were performed as shown in Fig. 4d. After 4 cyclic tests (20 h), the photocatalyst retained ∼ 87% of the initial photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation, indicating its good durability. And a slight decrease in the amount of hydrogen may be the result of sacrificial reagent consumption, indicating that it possesses sustainable photocatalytic activity and produce hydrogen over a long period of time [11]. Furthermore, the preservation of morphology and structure of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS after four consecutive cycles by XRD, SEM, TEM, and XPS measurement. XRD pattern of recycled catalyst displays that the crystal structure of the 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is well maintained before and after reaction (Fig. S8). Corresponding morphology from the SEM and TEM images that 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS still has a nanosheet structure, indicating that it has high stability (Fig. S9 and S10). From the high-resolution XPS spectrum of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, the valence states of Zn, In and S did not change after reaction, demonstrating the good irradiation stability of the 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (Fig. S11). EPR spectra (Fig. S12) were obtained before and after the photocatalytic reaction. Excitingly, the characteristic peak (g = 2.003) intensity of sulfur vacancies after photocatalytic reaction cycles was almost identical to that of fresh 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, suggesting that the sulfur vacancies are very stable during the photocatalytic process.

The transient photovoltage (TPV) tests have been widely carried out to investigate the charge transfer kinetics of sulfur vacancies and CDs on the interfaces of the photocatalysts [42,43]. In Fig. 5 a, photovoltage intensity of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is the highest than compared samples, indicating that more electrons in Vs-ZIS can be excited with the introduction of CDs. Furthermore, according to our previously reported work, due to the electron sink effect by the embedded CDs, the “trapped” charges cannot participate in the recombination process in a short period time, resulting in a negative signal in the TPV curve (the insert image in Fig. S13) [44]. Over time, the “trapped” charge slowly crosses the potential barrier and re-engages in the electron-hole recombination process, eventually reaching equilibrium. Furthermore, the decay constant (τ) was used to evaluate the electron-hole recombination process. The τ values is extended from 0.159 ms of ZIS to 0.255 ms of Vs-ZIS while the τ value of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS was 0.373 ms (Fig. 5b). This indicates that sulfur vacancies can trap the excited electrons, resulting in long carrier lifetime [45]. At the same time, the presence of CDs can lead to more electrons transfer to the surface, and inhibit the charge recombination. The charges extraction rate (tmax) is used to analyze the charge extraction process, the tmax2 (0.397) of ZIS is less than the tmax3 (0.446) of Vs-ZIS (Fig. 5c), which may be because the sulfur vacancies as electron trap reduces the charge extraction rate. However, after the addition of CDs, tmax4 (0.383) of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is the smallest, demonstrating that the introduction of CDs in Vs-ZIS greatly accelerates charge extraction process, which leads to the directional transfer of charge [46]. The maximum electron extraction amount of the sample can be calculated from integral area of shadow part A, corresponding to the charge extraction ability. The A of Vs-ZIS (A3 = 0.0518) is larger than that of ZIS (A2 = 0.0315), and proving that sulfur vacancies greatly improve the ability of photocatalyst to extract charges. After the introduction of CDs, the A of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (A4 = 0.0654) is the largest compared to Vs-ZIS and ZIS (Fig. 5d), which demonstrates that more electrons quickly transfer to the surface of CDs, indicating CDs as electron acceptors, and leading to the efficient charge carrier transfer and separation [47]. To effectively analyze charge transfer mechanism under light excitation, the effective charges concentrations (Aeff = A*τ/tmax) of photocatalyst was further calculated [48]. The Aeff of CDs is larger than that of ZIS, which was also caused by the electron sink effect of CDs that attracts more electrons (Fig. S14). And 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS possesses the largest Aeff, implying elevated charge migration behavior and promoted electron-hole separation after introducing CDs and sulfur vacancies. In summary, sulfur vacancies in the ZIS would capture the electrons, thereby prolonging the charge lifetime. In addition, the electron sink effect of CDs not only enhance the ability of Vs-ZIS to generate electrons, but also facilitate the directional migration of the photogenerated carriers between CDs and ZIS surface.

To explore the carrier kinetic behaviors in depth, fast Fourier transform (FFT) and continuous wavelet transform (CWT) were carried out based on the TPV curves. The FFT relaxation signals of ZIS, Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS showed a series of continuous signals with no visible peak, demonstrating the absence of significant static or periodic frequency components (Fig. 6 a). However, FFT can only identify the frequency components in these signals, it is unable to analyze the relationship between frequency and time [49]. CWT was used to convert non-static TPV curves into frequency-scale/time-scale [45], the 3D CWT patterns of samples are shown in Fig. S15. The interface electron transport can be analyzed by comparing the peaks of different samples at fixed frequencies. The peak intensity versus time at different frequencies elucidates the dynamic electron transfer process, where low (1–5 Hz) and high frequencies (>5 Hz) represent slow and fast electron transfer process, respectively [50]. At the low frequency, the peak time of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is longer than that of Vs-ZIS, which can be attributed to the electron sink effect of CDs to store charges, resulting in prolonged lifetime of the electrons at low frequencies (Fig. 6b and Fig. S16 a-c). At the high frequency, the peak time of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS gradually shifts to the left (Fig. 6c and Fig. S16 d-e). The time difference (Δt: t′ – t) at the fixed frequency between Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS were calculated, suggesting that CDs make the slow electron transfer slower and the fast electron transfer slightly faster (Fig. 6d). In addition, the position of the peak of ZIS is shorter than that of Vs-ZIS and 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS in the all frequency scale region, implying that sulfur vacancies act as the trap state to capture electrons to prolong the capture and accumulation of electrons lifetime. Based on all above results, due to the electron sink effect of CDs, thus the lifetime of low-frequency electrons is prolonged, and the migration of high-frequency electrons is accelerated, which is beneficial for the transfer of charge carriers from ZIS to CDs. Meanwhile, sulfur vacancies can trap electrons and further prevent electron-hole recombination.

The synergistic effect of CDs and sulfur vacancies on carriers’ separation and transfer in ZIS were further analyzed by photoelectrochemical characterizations. Time-resolved fluorescence decay spectroscopy (TRPL) was performed to obtain quantitative index of photogenerated charge transfer [51]. The decays curves shown in Fig. 7 a follow the quadratic exponential model (relevant dates are presented in Table S2). The average fluorescence lifetime (τA = 5.16 ns) of the Vs-ZIS is longer than that of ZIS (τA = 4.01 ns). The sulfur vacancies on the Vs-ZIS surface can capture electrons and prolong the charge carrier lifetime, thereby slowing the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Notably, the calculated average lifetime of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (τA = 5.38 ns) is longer than that of Vs-ZIS, which was due to reason that the introduction of CDs can accelerate interfacial charge separation and transfer kinetic, and increase the opportunity for photogenerated electrons to participate in protons reduction. Consistently, pure ZIS shows the strong photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) intensity in Fig. 7b. After introducing sulfur vacancies, PL intensity of Vs-ZIS significantly weakened, which indicates that sulfur vacancies can improve photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency [52]. This phenomenon can be further improved after the introduction of CDs in Vs-ZIS, demonstrating the improved photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency. In addition, the photocurrent intensity of 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS is higher than that of Vs-ZIS, ZIS and CDs, which means an improved charge separation efficiency in 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS (Fig. 7c). Furthermore, such properties are also measured using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests (Fig. 7d), the arc radius decreases in the following order: CDs > ZIS > Vs-ZIS > 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS, which indicates that 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS has excellent conductivity and causes faster charge transfer in 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS [53–55]. The above results of photoelectrochemical characterizations confirmed that the synergy effect of sulfur vacancies and CDs is capable of accelerating the charge transfer and separation, ultimately promote photocatalytic hydrogen production.

As discussed above, the mechanism of charge transfer and photocatalytic hydrogen production over CDs/Vs-ZIS photocatalyst is illustrated in Fig. 8 . Under light illumination, ZIS is excited to produce photogenerated electrons and holes, respectively. The sulfur vacancies serve as the electron trap to slow down the electron transfer and significantly prolong the lifetime of electrons. Due to the electron sink effect of CDs, the photogenerated electrons of Vs-ZIS are rapidly oriented transported to the CDs to drive the photoreduction of H+ on the surface of CDs to H2. Meanwhile, photogenerated holes can be rapidly captured by the sacrificial agent TEOA. In short, benefiting from the collaborative optimization of CDs in sulfur vacancy-tailored ZIS nanosheets, 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS exhibits superior photocatalytic hydrogen production performance.

In conclusion, we have explored an in situ coupling strategy to embed CDs on ultrathin ZnIn2S4 nanosheets with sulfur vacancies by a one-step solvothermal method. The TPV test unveiled that sulfur vacancies acted as electron traps to accelerate the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges, prolong the carrier lifetime, and emphasized the role of CDs serve as directional charge transfer channels in provoking charge redistribution. The optimized 2% CDs/Vs-ZIS photocatalyst showed remarkable photocatalytic activity with a hydrogen evolution rate of 5.93 mmol g−1h−1 at atmospheric pressure. This work provides a reasonable strategy for designing novel photocatalysts with efficient solar energy conversion from the angle of charge kinetics.

Kai Yu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zenan Li: Formal analysis. Tianyang Zhang: Formal analysis. Tiwei He: Formal analysis. Zhenglong Fan: Formal analysis. Hui Huang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Fan Liao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yang Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.
